lines: She does not turn from the fire. "You walk like the archives," she says. "Vael walked like that, when he was new."
A slow gesture to the stones across from her. "Sit if you like. Stand if you must."

choices.label: I'm Junior Cape Reed. And you?

actions.message: Not weariness — guilt. The kind that sits where breath should be. She has spent years rehearsing answers to a question no one ever came to ask. Now you're here, asking it.
Her face stays unreadable. She prefers it that way.

lines: She stirs the fire with a stick that used to be a chair-leg. "Ask. I'll tell you what I can."

choices.label: I'm Junior Cape Reed. And you?

lines: The smallest nod. "Cault. I wore the white once. They took it back."

choices.label: Then you'll know what's wrong with the Circuit.

lines: She looks at you for the first time. "It is what it is. Thank you for saying so — not many would."
"I refused a posting. The capital takes that personally." A breath. "Old story, long one. We've shorter problems tonight."

"They handed me a recertification to sign — for a stretch of circuit no Cape had set foot on in eleven years. Sign that the bindings were sound, they said. I knew they were rotting. I would not put my name to it." She looks past you, toward the open ground. "You crossed that same stretch to reach me. If they sent you, it was for the same reason — to make the file true again without doing the work the file claims was done."

"Because no one else came, and the file has to show someone did." A pause that is not unkind. "I don't blame you for being sent. I'd blame you for leaving without doing the thing the paperwork pretends you already did."

choices.label: Then tell me what the work is.

lines: She looks up. "The Reach Circuit is older than the Capes who keep it — older than the New Empire, by some readings. A ring of stones, set to hold the catacombs quiet." A breath. "They aren't quiet anymore."

choices.label: [Persuasion DC 12] What do they actually need?
What were they built to hold?

lines: "Maintenance." The same word the capital has used for a decade. She shrugs, slow. "I'm sorry — I don't have a better one to give the wrong audience."

choices.label: [Persuasion DC 12] Speak plainly. I'm not the capital.

lines: She sets the stick down. "They have to be renewed — by hand, by a living Cape standing at the stone. You trace the binding-rite back into the glyphwork and press your own seal to close it. That is what keeps a ward sound, and it is what a recertification is *meant* to be: a Cape, present, doing the work." Her mouth twists. "The capital stopped sending Capes who can perform the rite. So they send the stamp without the Cape. Paper, where there should be a person."

choices.label: Where would I find the rite?

lines: "Quietly. Politely. Yes." A pause. "It's cheaper to forge a stamp than to train a Cape. The signatures are real. The work behind them is not."

"Near the sanctum threshold. Half-buried in the masonry that came down with the roof. Brush the moss back — the face is still legible. Read it carefully. You'll need the first three lines of the rite."

"Age. Neglect. The two we can name, and a longer list we can't." She almost smiles. "The capital would rather have a villain than a mirror, so it writes *sabotage* in its letters. There's no villain. Only the Circuit, what it was built to hold, and the gap where the work used to be done."

choices.label: What was it built to hold?
Did Vael know all of this?

lines: She glances toward the dark mouth at the back of the sanctum. "What the Circuit was raised to contain. I won't name it on this side of the stones — a name gives a thing purchase." A long beat. "It stirs. The wards fail, and what they held remembers how to move."
"Less than the capital will believe. More than you'd fear — if you start tonight." She looks at you, evenly. "Long enough to learn the rite and reach the keystone. Not long enough to sleep on it."
Her voice softens by half a step. "I knew him long enough to know the Cape he became. Patient. A worrier. He read the same archives I did."

actions.message: She's choosing her words around grief, not secrecy. The pause before each sentence is for her, not for you.

choices.label: Did he know what the wards needed?

lines: "He worked it out before I did. A year in the lower archive, chasing every line the censors missed — until he understood what the stones really needed, and that no one was giving it to them."
"Then he came west. Stopped at this ruin to refill his lamp. Drank my tea. Wouldn't stay the night."

choices.label: Where did he go from here?

lines: "Twice. The first time, excited — the way new Capes are. The second time he carried a satchel of working notes and a lamp he'd refilled three times on the road." A breath. "He left both with me. I kept them."
She reaches behind her cloak and produces a worn satchel, hands it across the fire. "Yours, then. Take them. They'll spare you a season of reading you don't have."

choices.label: Did he know what he was walking into?

lines: Her voice drops — the way you step around an open grave. "Down. Into the catacombs. To the keystone — to hold the binding by hand, because the broken stones no longer carry the rite that far."
"Autumn. The first cold. He left the day the leaves turned."
"No."

choices.label: Has anyone gone in after him?
[Insight DC 14] Tell me what really happened.

lines: "No one was sent. No one volunteered. And I am not what I was."

She looks down at her hands a long moment before she speaks. "He went to hold the keystone himself. When a stone is too far gone to take the binding, the rite can't be worked *on* it anymore — it has to be held. A Cape stands in the breach and carries the binding with their own will, in the stone's place." A long, quiet pause. "He has been standing there since autumn. If he is still standing."

onEnter.text: I told the new one the truth about Vael. She did not turn away.

choices.label: What is the keystone holding back?
Then I need the way down.

lines: She nods, once. "Behind the sanctum. A trapdoor we used to keep clear — I have kept it clear. The first stair is loose. The second is honest."
"Half a day if you don't stop. Longer if you do the work properly on the way down — and you should. A ward you pass is a ward you can mend."
A small smile — the first you have seen. "I am very old. I'd only slow you. I'll keep the fire. If you come back up, the tea will be hot."

"Work the rite only at a stone — never in the open passages. Once you begin a binding, do not stop until it is sealed." Her eyes find yours. "And whatever you find down there with Vael — do not call it by name."

She rises, slow, and pulls back a sheet of canvas at the rear of the sanctum. A trapdoor — planks dark with age, an iron pull worn smooth. "Then go. No one will certify this for you. Whatever you do down there is yours alone — the capital has not sent anyone in three years, and I won't pretend they'll come if you fall." A pause. "Walk steady, Junior Cape."

onEnter.text: She did not turn away. I gave her the trap and the warning. May the work hold.
